meta.note: New project — no existing codebase. Verification focuses on external claims: packages, tools, APIs, and spec consistency.

findings.claim: mitmproxy >= 10.0
findings.detail: mitmproxy is real, on PyPI. Latest is 12.2.1 (released 2025-11-24). Version 10.0+ has been available since August 2023. Constraint '>= 10.0' is satisfied by current releases.

findings.claim: pystray >= 0.19
findings.detail: pystray is real, on PyPI. Latest is 0.19.5 (released 2023-09-17). Version 0.19+ is available. pystray supports Windows system tray with context menus and submenus via Icon, Menu, MenuItem classes.

findings.claim: Pillow >= 10.0
findings.detail: Pillow is real, on PyPI. Latest is 12.1.1. Version 10.0+ has been available since July 2023. Required by pystray for icon image handling.

findings.claim: requests >= 2.31
findings.detail: requests is real, on PyPI. Latest is 2.33.0. Version 2.31+ has been available. Used in health_check.py for HTTP calls.

findings.claim: pyinstaller >= 6.0
findings.detail: pyinstaller is real, on PyPI. Latest is 6.19.0. Version 6.0+ is available. Supports Python 3.8-3.14.

findings.claim: certutil.exe installs CA cert to CurrentUser store
findings.detail: certutil.exe is a real Windows built-in tool. The command 'certutil -addstore -user Root <cert.pem>' installs to the CurrentUser Root store. Crucially: adding to CurrentUser store does NOT require admin elevation, only LocalMachine store requires admin. The tech-spec claim that 'CA cert install may require admin confirmation' (from D5) is inaccurate for CurrentUser — it will succeed without UAC.

findings.claim: netsh.exe manages firewall rules for WebRTC STUN blocking
findings.detail: netsh.exe (specifically 'netsh advfirewall firewall add rule') is a real Windows built-in tool that can create per-application firewall rules blocking specific UDP ports. However: modifying Windows Firewall rules via netsh ALWAYS requires admin elevation (UAC prompt). The tech-spec states 'admin required at setup' (D3), which is correct, but this may conflict with NFR-1 in user-spec which says 'no admin rights' (with caveat 'допустимо: CA cert may require confirmation'). The user-spec does NOT explicitly list firewall rule creation as an admin-required exception.

findings.claim: winreg Python module for WinINET registry (HKCU)
findings.detail: winreg is a real Python standard library module on Windows. The registry path HKCU\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Internet Settings with values ProxyEnable (DWORD), ProxyServer (String) is the correct WinINET proxy configuration location. HKCU writes do NOT require admin elevation.

findings.claim: tkinter is built-in for setup wizard
findings.detail: tkinter is included in the Python standard library on Windows. No installation required. Suitable for native-looking Windows dialogs as claimed.

findings.claim: mitmproxy addon API: class with request/response hooks, flow.request.headers, flow.response.headers, flow.response.content
findings.detail: The mitmproxy addon API is real and works as described. Class-based addons with request(self, flow) and response(self, flow) hooks are the current official pattern. flow.request.headers and flow.response.headers provide dict-like header access. flow.response.content provides binary response body. mitmproxy handles gzip/brotli decompression automatically when accessing .content (as opposed to .raw_content).

findings.claim: mitmproxy handles TLS termination, HTTP/2, gzip/brotli decoding, certificate generation
findings.detail: All four capabilities are real and documented features of mitmproxy. Certificate generation happens automatically on first run into ~/.mitmproxy/ as stated in the Security Considerations section.

findings.claim: mitmproxy CA key stored in ~/.mitmproxy/
findings.detail: Confirmed: mitmproxy generates CA keys in ~/.mitmproxy/ by default on first run. The cert file is ~/.mitmproxy/mitmproxy-ca-cert.pem. This path is accurate.

findings.claim: JS injection overrides: Intl.DateTimeFormat, Date.getTimezoneOffset, Temporal.Now.timeZoneId, navigator.geolocation, navigator.language(s), RTCPeerConnection
findings.detail: Intl.DateTimeFormat, Date.getTimezoneOffset, navigator.geolocation, navigator.language/languages, and RTCPeerConnection are all valid browser APIs overridable via Object.defineProperty. HOWEVER: Temporal.Now.timeZoneId is a Temporal API feature that only reached Chrome in v145 (January/February 2026). As of the spec date (2026-03-27), Chrome 145+ users will have Temporal — the spec's inclusion of Temporal override is forward-looking and now relevant, but the tech-spec should acknowledge this is a new API and may not be present in older Chrome versions still in use.

findings.claim: Object.defineProperty makes overrides non-enumerable and harder to detect (D8)
findings.detail: Object.defineProperty is the correct and standard approach for stealthy API overrides. Setting configurable:false prevents re-override. Using IIFE pattern is correct for scope isolation. These are established anti-fingerprinting techniques used by tools like CreepJS test harnesses.

findings.claim: Firewall rules blocking UDP ports 3478, 5349, 19302-19309 for WebRTC STUN blocking
findings.detail: Ports 3478 and 5349 are the standard STUN/TURN ports (IANA registered). Port 19302 is confirmed as Google's primary STUN port. However, the range 19302-19309 is only partially confirmed: sources confirm 19302-19305 for Google Voice/WebRTC. Ports 19306-19309 are not confirmed in public documentation as active Google STUN ports. The range may be overly broad (blocking unused ports is harmless) or may miss actual ports. This is a low-severity concern.

findings.claim: PyInstaller --onedir starts instantly; --onefile has slow startup due to extraction (D7)
findings.detail: Confirmed. onefile bundles everything into a single exe that must extract to a temp directory on each launch (adding 2-3+ seconds). onedir creates a folder with exe + dependencies that loads nearly as fast as the original Python script. The design decision is well-founded.

findings.claim: FR-2 preset: US timezone America/New_York, coords 38.90 -77.04 (Washington DC), language en-US
findings.detail: America/New_York is a valid IANA timezone. Coordinates 38.90, -77.04 are accurate for Washington DC. en-US is the correct BCP47 language tag. Data is internally consistent.

findings.claim: FR-2 preset: DE timezone Europe/Berlin, coords 52.52 13.41 (Berlin), language de-DE
findings.detail: Europe/Berlin is a valid IANA timezone. Coordinates 52.52, 13.41 are accurate for Berlin, Germany. de-DE is the correct BCP47 language tag. Data is internally consistent.

findings.claim: FR-2 preset: NL timezone Europe/Amsterdam, coords 52.37 4.90 (Amsterdam), language nl-NL
findings.detail: Europe/Amsterdam is a valid IANA timezone. Coordinates 52.37, 4.90 are accurate for Amsterdam, Netherlands. nl-NL is the correct BCP47 language tag. Data is internally consistent.

findings.claim: FR-2 preset: GB timezone Europe/London, coords 51.51 -0.13 (London), language en-GB
findings.detail: Europe/London is a valid IANA timezone. Coordinates 51.51, -0.13 are accurate for London, UK. en-GB is the correct BCP47 language tag. Data is internally consistent.

findings.claim: D2 (tech-spec): Chrome/Edge only, no Firefox. NFR-3 (user-spec): works for any browser (Chrome, Edge, Firefox) and desktop apps using system proxy.
findings.detail: The user-spec NFR-3 explicitly states the tool should work for Chrome, Edge, Firefox, and desktop apps using system proxy. The tech-spec D2 explicitly removes Firefox support, citing NSS CA store and proxy configuration complexity. The user-spec 'Limitations' section does note 'Firefox requires separate proxy and CA setup', suggesting the user may have accepted this. However, NFR-3 is stated as a non-functional requirement, not a limitation. The tech-spec does not document that this was a deliberate scope reduction from NFR-3. This inconsistency should be explicitly acknowledged and reconciled in the tech-spec.
findings.recommendation: Add a note in D2 that explicitly references NFR-3 and records the decision to scope down, or update the user-spec to remove Firefox from NFR-3.

findings.claim: D5/NFR-1: Setup wizard handles CA cert install 'with explanation'; NFR-1 says CA cert install 'may require admin confirmation' but firewall rules (which DO require admin) are not called out as an admin-required exception in user-spec.
findings.detail: The tech-spec D3 is clear that firewall rules require admin elevation (UAC). The user-spec NFR-1 says 'допустимо: CA cert may require confirmation' but does NOT list firewall rule creation as an acceptable admin-required step. Since certutil to CurrentUser Root does NOT require admin (verified: F-06), the only actually admin-requiring operation is netsh firewall rules. The user-spec NFR-1 is therefore more restrictive than the tech-spec assumes. The user-spec limitations section mentions 'WebRTC via JS less reliable but no admin required' which suggests the user may prefer a no-admin WebRTC approach. The tech-spec chose firewall rules (admin required) over JS-only (no admin), and this preference is not validated against the user-spec.
findings.recommendation: Confirm with user that admin elevation for firewall rule creation (netsh) is acceptable, and explicitly add it as an exception in NFR-1 in the user-spec, or fall back to JS-only WebRTC override.

verified_claims_summary: All 5 Python packages (mitmproxy, pystray, Pillow, requests, pyinstaller) exist on PyPI at the specified minimum versions
certutil.exe and netsh.exe are real Windows built-in tools with the described capabilities
winreg Python module exists and the WinINET registry path/values are correct
tkinter is a real Python built-in (no installation needed)
mitmproxy addon API (class-based, request/response hooks) works as described
All 4 country presets have internally consistent IANA timezone, GPS coordinates, and BCP47 language tags
PyInstaller onedir startup performance advantage over onefile is real and documented
Object.defineProperty override technique is valid and appropriate for the stated goal
warnings_summary: certutil to CurrentUser Root does NOT require admin elevation (contrary to D5's implication) — this is actually GOOD NEWS for NFR-1
netsh firewall rules ALWAYS require admin elevation — only partially covered in user-spec NFR-1
Google STUN port range 19302-19309 is only confirmed to 19302-19305; 19306-19309 are unconfirmed
Temporal.Now.timeZoneId only available in Chrome 145+ (Jan 2026); older Chrome will not have it — graceful fallback needed
Firefox scope reduction (D2) is not reconciled against NFR-3 in user-spec
contradictions_summary: NFR-3 (user-spec) requires Firefox support; D2 (tech-spec) drops Firefox — not explicitly reconciled
NFR-1 (user-spec) only calls out CA cert as acceptable admin step; netsh firewall rules (always admin) are not listed as an acceptable exception
